metadata.summary: Build Factory Boy DjangoModelFactory classes for Django test data: SubFactory, Faker, post_generation, and pytest-factoryboy registration patterns.

content.title: Django pytest Factories
content.summary: pytest-factoryboy bridges Factory Boy DjangoModelFactory classes with pytest fixtures, eliminating manual test data creation. Define factories in tests/factories/, use SubFactory for FK relationships, Faker for realistic data, and register in conftest.py to auto-create singular and batch fixtures.

why.p: Factories decouple test data creation from test logic. Instead of hand-crafting model instances in every test (which duplicates schema knowledge and breaks when fields are added), factories encode the construction rules once. pytest-factoryboy auto-creates two fixtures per registered factory — a singular instance and a _factory callable for batch creation — reducing boilerplate to near zero. SubFactory and Faker make factories self-maintaining as schemas evolve.

list.item: Creating test data for Django models in any pytest test.
Tests that need consistent, realistic data without hard-coding values.
Setting up related-object graphs (User → Profile → Order → OrderItem).
Batch-creating multiple instances for list/filter tests.
Replacing manual User.objects.create() calls in test setUp or fixtures.
Simple, one-off tests where a factory adds more complexity than value.
Tests that specifically assert on hard-coded values — pass the values explicitly to the factory instead of using Faker defaults.
Performance benchmarks where factory overhead matters — use bulk_create directly.

section.name: basic-factory-definition
section.p: Every Django model used in tests should have a corresponding factory in tests/factories/. The factory encodes the minimum required fields, with realistic defaults from Faker and correct FK wiring via SubFactory.

rule.statement: Define Django model factories in tests/factories/ using factory.django.DjangoModelFactory. Use factory.SubFactory for foreign keys. Use factory.Faker for realistic data. Use @factory.post_generation for M2M relationships and password hashing. Include skip_postgeneration_save=True in Meta for Django 4.2+ to avoid double save on post_generation. Register every factory in root conftest.py via pytest_factoryboy.register().
rule.rationale: DjangoModelFactory handles database save and cleanup. SubFactory creates dependent objects automatically and resolves circular dependencies. Faker ensures test data looks realistic (valid emails, real names) without hard-coded values. post_generation covers cases factory fields cannot express, such as M2M adds and password hashing. pytest-factoryboy.register auto-creates user and user_factory fixtures from UserFactory with no extra code.

example.title: User factory with post_generation
example.code:
# tests/factories/users.py import factory from factory import fuzzy from apps.users.models import User, Profile
class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = User skip_postgeneration_save = True # Django 4.2+ optimization
email = factory.Faker("email") name = factory.Faker("name") user_type = "regular" is_active = True
@factory.post_generation def password(self, create, extracted, **kwargs): # Use simple password for tests self.set_password(extracted or "testpass123") if create: self.save(update_fields=["password"])
class AdminUserFactory(UserFactory): user_type = "admin" is_staff = True is_superuser = True
class ProfileFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = Profile
user = factory.SubFactory(UserFactory) bio = factory.Faker("text", max_nb_chars=200) avatar = factory.django.ImageField(width=100, height=100)

example.title: Order factory with SubFactory and post_generation items

section.name: pytest-factoryboy-registration
section.p: pytest-factoryboy.register() converts factory classes into pytest fixtures automatically, making factories available across the entire test suite with no extra plumbing.

rule.statement: Register all factories in root conftest.py using pytest_factoryboy.register(). register(UserFactory) creates two fixtures: 'user' (singular model instance) and 'user_factory' (callable for batch or custom creation). Use _name parameter to register multiple factories of the same model under different names (e.g., register(AdminUserFactory, _name="admin_user")). Do NOT manually define @pytest.fixture def user if you have registered UserFactory — silent override will occur.
rule.rationale: pytest-factoryboy eliminates fixture boilerplate: no @pytest.fixture def user(db): return UserFactory() in every conftest. The auto-created fixtures request the db fixture automatically, so tests just declare 'user' as a parameter and get a saved User instance. The _name parameter avoids collisions when registering multiple factories for the same model.

example.title: Factory registration in conftest.py
example.code:
# tests/conftest.py from pytest_factoryboy import register from tests.factories.users import UserFactory, AdminUserFactory, ProfileFactory from tests.factories.orders import ProductFactory, OrderFactory, OrderItemFactory
# Register creates 'user' and 'user_factory' fixtures automatically register(UserFactory) register(AdminUserFactory, _name="admin_user") # creates 'admin_user' and 'admin_user_factory' register(ProfileFactory) register(ProductFactory) register(OrderFactory) register(OrderItemFactory)
# Usage in tests - no extra fixture code needed: @pytest.mark.django_db def test_user_creation(user): assert user.email is not None assert user.pk is not None
@pytest.mark.django_db def test_create_batch(user_factory): users = user_factory.create_batch(3, user_type="admin") assert len(users) == 3 assert all(u.user_type == "admin" for u in users)

antipattern.description: Manually writing @pytest.fixture def user(db): return UserFactory() after registering UserFactory with pytest-factoryboy.
antipattern.reason: pytest-factoryboy already created a 'user' fixture. The manual fixture silently overrides it without warning. All tests using 'user' now get the manual fixture, which may have different behavior (wrong scope, no cleanup). Use only one source per fixture name.

section.name: factory-patterns
section.p: Practical factory patterns for common Django testing scenarios.

section.name: string-reference-models
section.p: Use string references in Meta.model when there is a risk of circular imports between factory modules.

example.title: Factories using string model references to avoid circular imports
example.code:
# tests/factories/orders.py — string references avoid circular imports import factory from decimal import Decimal
class ProductFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): """Factory for products."""
class Meta: model = "products.Product" # String reference
name = factory.Faker("word") description = factory.Faker("sentence") price = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyDecimal(10.00, 500.00, precision=2) stock = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyInteger(1, 100) is_active = True
class OrderFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): """Factory for orders."""
class Meta: model = "orders.Order"
user = factory.SubFactory("tests.factories.users.UserFactory") status = "pending" shipping_address = factory.Faker("address")
@factory.post_generation def items(self, create, extracted, **kwargs): """Create order items if specified.""" if not create or not extracted: return for item_data in extracted: OrderItemFactory(order=self, **item_data)
class OrderItemFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): """Factory for order items."""
class Meta: model = "orders.OrderItem"
order = factory.SubFactory(OrderFactory) product = factory.SubFactory(ProductFactory) quantity = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyInteger(1, 5) unit_price = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda o: o.product.price)

example.title: Factory module __init__.py for clean imports
example.code:
# tests/factories/__init__.py """ Test factories module. Import all factories here for easy access. """ from .users import UserFactory, AdminUserFactory, ProfileFactory from .orders import ProductFactory, OrderFactory, OrderItemFactory
__all__ = [ "UserFactory", "AdminUserFactory", "ProfileFactory", "ProductFactory", "OrderFactory", "OrderItemFactory", ]

section.name: lazy-attribute-pattern
section.p: Use factory.LazyAttribute when a field value depends on other fields of the same object. The lambda receives the object being built.

example.title: LazyAttribute for derived fields
example.code:
class OrderItemFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = OrderItem
product = factory.SubFactory(ProductFactory) quantity = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyInteger(1, 5) # unit_price mirrors product.price at factory time unit_price = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda o: o.product.price) # subtotal derives from quantity * unit_price subtotal = factory.LazyAttribute(lambda o: o.quantity * o.unit_price)

section.name: mute-signals
section.p: Factory Boy model creation can trigger post_save signals (sending emails, starting Celery tasks). These side effects interfere with unit tests that do not test the signal. Mute them selectively.

rule.statement: Use factory.django.mute_signals(post_save) as a decorator on factory classes or test functions when the factory creates a model that triggers email sending, Celery task enqueuing, or other side effects via post_save signals. Verify the side effect in separate, dedicated tests that specifically test the signal handler.
rule.rationale: Unmuted signals make unrelated tests fail when the email backend or Celery queue is not configured for them. Muting signals in the factory keeps test scope focused. Separate signal tests verify the handler independently of factory-creation tests.

example.title: Muting signals in factory
example.code:
import factory from django.db.models.signals import post_save from apps.users.models import User
@factory.django.mute_signals(post_save) class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): """UserFactory with muted post_save signals.
Use when post_save sends welcome emails or triggers Celery tasks that are not relevant to the test being run. """ class Meta: model = User skip_postgeneration_save = True
email = factory.Faker("email") name = factory.Faker("name")
# For one-off muting in a specific test: @pytest.mark.django_db def test_model_fields_only(user_factory): with factory.django.mute_signals(post_save): user = user_factory() assert user.email is not None

section.name: common-pitfalls
section.p: Known factory-related failure modes in Django test suites.

antipattern.description: Factory Boy + Faker unique values drift: tests asserting that response.data["email"] equals "user@example.com" fail when Faker generates a different email.
antipattern.reason: Faker generates random data on every run. Never assert on the literal value Faker will produce. Instead, assert on the factory instance's own attribute: assert response.data["email"] == user.email. Always derive expected values from the object, not from the Faker call.

antipattern.description: bulk_create skipping signals: factories using bulk_create don't trigger post_save, so tests depending on signals see no side effect.
antipattern.reason: Django's bulk_create bypasses the model save method and all signals. Tests that expect a Celery task to be enqueued or an audit record to be created via post_save will silently not see it when bulk_create is used. Use individual save() or the factory's create() method when signals matter.

antipattern.description: skip_postgeneration_save missing for Django 4.2+ factories with @factory.post_generation methods.
antipattern.reason: Without skip_postgeneration_save=True in Meta, Factory Boy calls save() after every post_generation hook, causing redundant DB writes. Add skip_postgeneration_save=True and manually save inside the post_generation function only when needed.

checklist.check: Factory created for every Django model used in tests
SubFactory used for all FK relationships (not direct model creation inside factory)
factory.Faker used for realistic data (email, name, address)
factory.LazyAttribute used for fields derived from other fields
skip_postgeneration_save=True in Meta for factories with post_generation hooks (Django 4.2+)
All factories registered in root conftest.py via pytest_factoryboy.register()
AdminUserFactory registered with _name="admin_user" to avoid name collision
No manual @pytest.fixture def user defined when UserFactory is registered
mute_signals used for factories that trigger email/Celery side effects
Tests assert on factory instance attributes, not on literal Faker-generated values
